skill.name: project-scaffolding
skill.description: Project scaffolding wizard for creating configured starter projects across web, mobile, backend, desktop, extension, serverless, library, CLI, and monorepo stacks. It guides framework choices and can generate files for common tooling such as TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, testing, Docker, GitHub Actions, database configuration, and environment templates.
skill.summary: Create configured starter projects for many modern application stacks.
skill.author: hmohamed01

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many high-severity hits, but most are false positives from markdown command examples, generated placeholder configuration, and framework reference text. No evidence found of malicious intent, prompt injection, credential exfiltration, or live network calls. The remaining concern is legitimate but elevated: the Python scaffolder creates many files under a path derived from the user-supplied project name without containment validation.

medium_findings.title: Unvalidated Project Output Path
medium_findings.description: The CLI stores args.name directly in ProjectConfig and create_project builds project_path as base_path / config.name. Because no normalization or containment check is shown, a name containing path separators could create files outside the intended working directory. This appears to be an input validation weakness in a legitimate scaffolding tool, not malicious behavior.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct evidence shows the output path is derived from a CLI argument and then created. The risk depends on how the skill is invoked, but the missing validation is clear.

low_findings.title: Documentation Command Examples Flagged as Execution
low_findings.description: Many external command findings are markdown examples or generated README instructions for users to run after scaffolding. The script imports subprocess, but no subprocess invocation was found in the reviewed script.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are imports, documentation, or generated instructions rather than executed shell calls. A targeted search found no subprocess usage in the Python script.

low_findings.title: Placeholder Environment and Secret References
low_findings.description: Environment and credential hits are generated .env.example placeholders or best-practice documentation telling users not to commit secrets. No evidence found that the skill reads local secret files or sends credentials anywhere.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The context shows placeholder templates and security guidance, not secret harvesting. The finding remains low because generated projects may include insecure placeholder defaults if users do not rotate them.

low_findings.title: Static Heuristic Combination Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The critical heuristic combined filesystem, network, and credential signals from different contexts. Review found file generation, localhost or documentation URLs, and placeholder environment values, but no combined exfiltration path.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations explain or demonstrate benign contexts for the combined signals. No evidence found of network calls paired with credential collection.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Controlled Filesystem Target
dangerous_patterns.description: The scaffolder creates the project directory from config.name and then writes many files under that directory. This pattern needs path normalization and an allowlisted project-name format before publication.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The write helpers are normal for scaffolding, but the base path depends on unvalidated user input. This makes the filesystem behavior security-relevant.

content.value_statement: Starting a project often requires repeated setup for frameworks, tooling, tests, and deployment files. This skill guides choices and generates a ready starter structure for many common stacks.
actual_capabilities: Guides users through project type, language, database, testing, and deployment choices.
Generates starter files for web, backend, desktop, extension, library, CLI, and monorepo projects.
Creates common configuration files such as package manifests, TypeScript settings, README files, and gitignore files.
Adds optional tooling patterns for linting, formatting, testing, Docker, and GitHub Actions.
Provides reference material for framework structures, best practices, and wizard options.
Includes a Python scaffolding script for custom project generation when native CLIs are not suitable.
limitations: Generated projects still need dependency installation and verification by the user.
The scaffolding script should be run only in directories where file creation is expected.
Project names should be reviewed because the script does not show strict path containment checks.
Framework versions and package recommendations may need updates over time.

use_cases.title: Start a modern web app
use_cases.description: Create a React, Next.js, Vue, Svelte, Astro, or Angular starter with selected styling and testing tools.

use_cases.title: Set up an API service
use_cases.description: Generate a FastAPI, Django, Express, NestJS, Go, Rust, or Java backend with database and quality tooling choices.

use_cases.title: Standardize team starters
use_cases.description: Create consistent starter projects with README files, environment examples, linting, tests, Docker, and CI patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Website
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a static website called portfolio-site with responsive HTML and CSS.

prompt_templates.title: Frontend App
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a React project called admin-dashboard with TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, ESLint, Prettier, and Vitest.

prompt_templates.title: Backend Service
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a FastAPI project called billing-api with SQLAlchemy, PostgreSQL settings, pytest, Ruff, Docker, and GitHub Actions.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced Workspace
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Turborepo workspace called commerce-platform with a Next.js app, shared TypeScript packages, testing, Docker, and CI configuration.

output_examples.input: Create a React project called client-portal with TypeScript and Tailwind CSS.
output: A client-portal project folder is created.
The project includes a package manifest, Vite configuration, TypeScript settings, source folders, Tailwind setup, tests, README, and gitignore.
The response includes next steps for installing dependencies and starting development.

output_examples.input: Create a FastAPI service called inventory-api with PostgreSQL and Docker.
output: An inventory-api backend starter is generated.
The project includes application folders, dependency files, environment examples, database configuration placeholders, Docker files, tests, and documentation.
The response explains the commands a user should run after reviewing the generated files.

output_examples.input: Create a monorepo for a web app and shared packages.
output: A workspace structure is created with application and package directories.
The project includes workspace configuration, build scripts, README guidance, and common quality tooling patterns.

best_practices: Use simple project names without path separators before running the scaffolding script.
Review generated environment examples and replace all placeholder values before deployment.
Run dependency installation, linting, and tests immediately after scaffolding to catch outdated packages or template drift.
anti_patterns: Do not run the script in a sensitive directory or with untrusted project names.
Do not treat generated secret placeholders as production-ready credentials.
Do not assume every generated framework recommendation is current without checking package versions.

faq.question: What does this skill create?
faq.answer: It creates starter project structures, configuration files, README files, environment examples, and optional tooling files for many application types.

faq.question: Does it install dependencies?
faq.answer: No. It generates files and gives follow-up commands. Users install dependencies after reviewing the generated project.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Is it safe to run automatically?
faq.answer: Use caution. The script writes many files and should be run only with trusted project names in an intended workspace.

faq.question: Can it create backend projects?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes options for Python, JavaScript, TypeScript, Go, Rust, Java, and other backend starter patterns.

faq.question: Can generated projects be customized?
faq.answer: Yes. The generated files are standard project files that users can edit after scaffolding.
